headline: Bantuan Sara Hidup Cash Transfers Reach 8.2M Recipients
context: The expanded Bantuan Sara Hidup Rakyat programme disbursed RM12.4 billion to 8.2 million recipients in 2025, with maximum individual payments rising to RM3,100 annually, while economists debate whether direct transfers address structural poverty drivers.

cards.big: RM12.4 billion in cash transfers to 8.2 million people — 25% of the population receives direct government handouts annually
cards.sub: RM3,100 maximum payment equals RM258 monthly — less than 15% of a living wage in most Malaysian cities

cards.big: Cash transfer spending tripled from RM4.2 billion in 2018 to RM12.4 billion — fastest growing budget line item after pensions
cards.sub: At current growth rate, BSH-type transfers will consume RM22 billion by 2030 — unsustainable without GST or new revenue

cards.big: 72% of recipients report spending transfers entirely on food and utilities — no capacity for savings or investment
cards.sub: Transfers prevent starvation but don't lift anyone out of poverty — B40 exit rate unchanged at 2.1% annually

cards.big: eKasih poverty database used for eligibility has 840,000 outdated records — people who died or moved still listed as recipients
cards.sub: 3 separate verification systems across Treasury, Welfare Department, and LHDN don't share data in real time

cards.big: Cash transfers keep people alive but not advancing — RM12.4 billion spent annually with zero measurable poverty exit impact is a permanent subsidy, not a solution
Should 30% of BSH budget be redirected to skills training and micro-enterprise grants with poverty exit milestones?
